_meta.description: Consolidated disease profiles extracted from persona_generator.py and dialogue_builder.py

differential_questions: Have you noticed increased thirst or urination recently?
Have you had any episodes of blurred vision?
Has your appetite changed unexpectedly?
Have you experienced any tingling or numbness in your hands or feet?
type 2 diabetes.patient_education: Monitor your blood sugar regularly, watch your carbohydrate intake, and check your feet daily for any sores or changes

differential_questions: Do you get headaches, especially in the morning?
Have you noticed any visual changes or spots in your vision?
Do you experience palpitations or racing heartbeat?
Has anyone in your family had kidney problems?
hypertension.patient_education: Reduce salt intake, exercise regularly, and take your blood pressure medication even when you feel fine — high blood pressure often has no symptoms

differential_questions: Have you had a fever or felt like you were getting sick recently?
Has your breathing gotten worse over the past day or two?
Have you had any recent pain crises or hospitalizations?
Are you coughing up anything, or is the cough dry?
Have you been taking your hydroxyurea regularly?
sickle cell anemia.patient_education: Stay well-hydrated, avoid extreme temperatures, and seek medical attention immediately if you develop fever, chest pain, or difficulty breathing — these could signal a sickle cell crisis

copd.patient_education: Quit smoking if you haven't already, use your inhalers as directed, and get your annual flu and pneumonia vaccines

chronic kidney disease.patient_education: Stay hydrated but follow your fluid restriction if given, avoid NSAIDs like ibuprofen, and monitor your blood pressure closely

differential_questions: Have you noticed any blood in your stool or very dark stools?
Do you get dizzy when standing up quickly?
Have you been craving unusual things like ice or dirt?
Has your diet changed recently, or have you been eating less meat?
anemia.patient_education: Take iron supplements with vitamin C to help absorption, avoid taking them with dairy or antacids, and let us know if you feel dizzy or unusually weak

differential_questions: Do you wake up at night feeling short of breath?
Have you noticed swelling in your ankles or legs?
Do you find you need more pillows to sleep comfortably?
Has your weight changed unexpectedly?
heart failure.patient_education: Weigh yourself daily — sudden weight gain can mean fluid retention. Limit salt and fluids, and call us if you notice increased swelling or shortness of breath

differential_questions: Have you been exposed to any new allergens or irritants?
Does the wheezing get worse at night or early morning?
How often are you using your rescue inhaler?
Have you had any recent respiratory infections?
asthma.patient_education: Use your inhaler as prescribed, avoid known triggers, and seek help immediately if your rescue inhaler isn't helping

differential_questions: Have you had a fever or chills?
Are you coughing up any phlegm? What color is it?
Have you been around anyone who was sick recently?
Do you feel pain when you take a deep breath?
pneumonia.patient_education: Complete the full course of antibiotics even if you feel better, stay hydrated, and seek care if your fever returns or breathing worsens

differential_questions: How has your mood been over the past few weeks?
Have you lost interest in activities you used to enjoy?
How has your sleep been — trouble falling or staying asleep?
Have you had any thoughts of hurting yourself?
depression.patient_education: The medication may take 2-4 weeks to take full effect. Don't stop taking it suddenly, and reach out immediately if you have any thoughts of self-harm

differential_questions: Have you noticed any yellowing of your skin or eyes?
Has your abdomen been feeling swollen or bloated?
Have you noticed any confusion or difficulty concentrating?
Have you had any unusual bleeding — nosebleeds, easy bruising?
cirrhosis.patient_education: Avoid alcohol entirely, limit salt intake, and watch for confusion, yellowing of skin/eyes, or abdominal swelling — these need urgent attention

differential_questions: Have you been feeling unusually cold even when others are comfortable?
Has your skin become dry or your hair thinner?
Have you been constipated more than usual?
Have you noticed any swelling in your neck?
hypothyroidism.patient_education: Take your thyroid medication on an empty stomach at the same time each day, and don't stop even if you feel better — this is typically a lifelong condition

differential_questions: Did the symptoms come on suddenly or gradually?
Have you had any weakness on one side of your body?
Have you had any difficulty speaking or understanding speech?
Have you had any vision changes?
stroke.patient_education: Seek emergency care immediately if you develop sudden weakness, speech difficulty, or vision loss. Take antiplatelet medication as prescribed and control blood pressure.

differential_questions: Have you noticed your heart racing or beating irregularly?
Have you had any episodes of dizziness or fainting?
Do you get short of breath with the palpitations?
Have you had any chest pain with these episodes?
atrial fibrillation.patient_education: Take blood thinners as prescribed to reduce stroke risk. Avoid excessive alcohol and caffeine. Seek care if palpitations are accompanied by chest pain or difficulty breathing.

differential_questions: Which joints have been affected? Is it the big toe?
Does the pain come on suddenly, especially at night?
Have you had these attacks before?
Do you consume alcohol or foods high in purines regularly?
gout.patient_education: Reduce alcohol and purine-rich foods (red meat, shellfish). Stay well hydrated. Take allopurinol regularly to prevent attacks, not just during flares.

differential_questions: Have you had any fractures from minimal trauma?
Have you noticed any loss of height or developing a stooped posture?
Have you been taking corticosteroids long-term?
Is there a family history of osteoporosis or hip fractures?
osteoporosis.patient_education: Ensure adequate calcium and vitamin D intake. Weight-bearing exercise helps maintain bone density. Take bisphosphonates on an empty stomach and remain upright for 30 minutes.

differential_questions: Do you get a burning sensation in your chest, especially after meals?
Does it worsen when you lie down or bend over?
Have you noticed any difficulty or pain when swallowing?
Do you experience regurgitation of food or sour liquid?
GERD.patient_education: Avoid trigger foods (spicy, fatty, acidic), eat smaller meals, and don't lie down within 3 hours of eating. Elevate the head of your bed if symptoms are worse at night.

differential_questions: Do you have burning or gnawing pain in your upper abdomen?
Does the pain improve or worsen with eating?
Have you noticed any black, tarry stools?
Do you take NSAIDs like ibuprofen or aspirin regularly?
peptic ulcer disease.patient_education: Complete the full antibiotic course if H. pylori positive. Avoid NSAIDs and alcohol. Take PPIs as prescribed. Seek immediate care if you vomit blood or pass black stools.

differential_questions: Is the headache one-sided or pulsating?
Do you have nausea or sensitivity to light and sound with the headaches?
Do you see visual disturbances like flashing lights before the headache starts?
How often do you get these headaches?
migraine.patient_education: Identify and avoid triggers (stress, certain foods, sleep changes). Use triptans early in an attack. If headaches are frequent, preventive medication may help.

differential_questions: Can you describe what happens during one of these episodes?
Do you have any warning before the episode starts?
Have you ever lost consciousness or had muscle jerking?
How long do the episodes typically last?
epilepsy.patient_education: Take seizure medication consistently — never stop abruptly. Avoid sleep deprivation and alcohol excess. Do not drive until cleared by your doctor.

differential_questions: Have you noticed a tremor, especially in your hand at rest?
Have your movements become slower or stiffer?
Have you had any changes in your handwriting — is it smaller?
Have you noticed any changes in your sense of smell?
Parkinson disease.patient_education: Take levodopa on time every day. Exercise regularly to maintain mobility. Watch for medication side effects like involuntary movements.

differential_questions: Which joints are affected? Are the symptoms symmetric on both sides?
Is there prolonged morning stiffness lasting more than an hour?
Are the joints swollen, warm, or tender?
Have you noticed any joint deformities?
rheumatoid arthritis.patient_education: Take DMARDs like methotrexate regularly — they slow disease progression. Gentle exercise helps maintain joint function. Report any signs of infection promptly.

differential_questions: How long have you been feeling excessively worried or anxious?
Do you have trouble controlling your worry?
Have you experienced physical symptoms like racing heart, sweating, or shortness of breath?
Has the anxiety affected your daily activities, work, or relationships?
anxiety disorder.patient_education: SSRIs may take 2-4 weeks to take full effect. Don't stop suddenly. Consider therapy (CBT) alongside medication. Regular exercise and good sleep help reduce anxiety.

differential_questions: Have you been feeling unusually hot or sweating more than usual?
Have you lost weight without trying?
Has your heart been racing or beating irregularly?
Have you noticed tremor in your hands or trouble sleeping?
hyperthyroidism.patient_education: Take antithyroid medication regularly. Avoid iodine-rich foods. Report sore throat or fever immediately (could indicate low white blood cells).

differential_questions: Do you have severe pain in your flank or lower abdomen that comes in waves?
Does the pain radiate to your groin?
Have you noticed any blood in your urine?
Have you had kidney stones before?
nephrolithiasis.patient_education: Drink at least 2-3 liters of water daily. Reduce sodium and animal protein. Strain your urine to catch any passed stones for analysis.

medications.name: rivaroxaban
medications.frequency: twice daily for 21 days then 20mg daily

differential_questions: Did the shortness of breath come on suddenly?
Have you had any recent leg swelling or pain?
Have you been immobile for a long period (surgery, long flight)?
Have you coughed up any blood?
pulmonary embolism.patient_education: Take anticoagulants exactly as prescribed. Avoid activities with high bleeding risk. Seek emergency care for sudden worsening of breathing.

differential_questions: How long have you had this cough?
Are you coughing up any phlegm? What color?
Have you had a fever?
Have you been around anyone who was sick?
bronchitis.patient_education: Most acute bronchitis is viral and doesn't need antibiotics. Rest, stay hydrated, and use a humidifier. See a doctor if cough lasts more than 3 weeks or you develop high fever.

differential_questions: Do you snore loudly or has anyone observed you stop breathing during sleep?
Do you feel excessively tired during the day despite adequate sleep time?
Do you wake up with morning headaches?
Do you have difficulty concentrating or feel foggy?
sleep apnea.patient_education: Use CPAP consistently every night. Weight loss can significantly improve symptoms. Avoid alcohol and sedatives before bed.

differential_questions: Is the affected area red, warm, swollen, and tender?
Do you have a fever?
Is the redness spreading?
Do you have any breaks in the skin — cuts, insect bites, or ulcers?
cellulitis.patient_education: Complete the full course of antibiotics. Elevate the affected limb. Mark the border of redness with a pen to track spread. Seek emergency care if redness spreads rapidly or you develop high fever.

differential_questions: Have you been exposed to anyone with COVID-19?
Do you have fever, cough, or loss of taste or smell?
Are you experiencing any shortness of breath?
Have you been vaccinated against COVID-19?
COVID-19.patient_education: Isolate to prevent spread. Monitor oxygen levels if you have a pulse oximeter. Seek emergency care for difficulty breathing, persistent chest pain, or confusion.

differential_questions: Do you have a severe headache, neck stiffness, and fever?
Are you sensitive to light?
Have you noticed any rash?
Have you had nausea, vomiting, or confusion?
meningitis.patient_education: Meningitis is a medical emergency. Seek immediate care if you develop severe headache with fever and neck stiffness.

differential_questions: Have you noticed yellowing of your skin or eyes?
Have you had dark urine or pale stools?
Do you have pain in the upper right side of your abdomen?
Have you had any known exposure — blood products, needles, or unprotected sex?
hepatitis B.patient_education: Avoid alcohol entirely. Take antiviral medication as prescribed. Get regular liver function tests and screening for liver cancer.

differential_questions: Do you have difficulty starting urination or a weak stream?
Do you wake up multiple times at night to urinate?
Do you feel like your bladder doesn't empty completely?
Have you had any urinary urgency or leakage?
benign prostatic hyperplasia.patient_education: Take alpha-blockers as prescribed. Reduce fluid intake before bedtime. Report any blood in urine or inability to urinate immediately.

differential_questions: Do you have abdominal pain that improves after a bowel movement?
Have you noticed changes in your stool frequency or form?
Do your symptoms worsen with stress?
Have you had any unintentional weight loss or blood in your stool?
irritable bowel syndrome.patient_education: IBS is a chronic but manageable condition. Identify trigger foods. Stress management and regular exercise can help. Fiber supplements may help constipation-predominant IBS.

differential_questions: Do you have severe upper abdominal pain that radiates to your back?
Does the pain worsen after eating, especially fatty foods?
Have you had nausea and vomiting?
Do you consume alcohol regularly?
pancreatitis.patient_education: Avoid alcohol completely. Follow a low-fat diet. Stay hydrated. Seek emergency care if pain becomes severe or you develop fever.

differential_questions: Have you had episodes of vision loss, numbness, or weakness that come and go?
Do your symptoms worsen with heat or exercise?
Have you had any bladder or bowel control problems?
Have you experienced unusual fatigue?
multiple sclerosis.patient_education: Disease-modifying therapy can slow progression. Avoid excessive heat. Stay active with gentle exercise. Report new neurological symptoms promptly.

differential_questions: Have you had a butterfly-shaped rash on your face?
Do your joints ache and swell?
Have you been unusually sensitive to sunlight?
Have you had any kidney problems or unexplained swelling?
systemic lupus erythematosus.patient_education: Take hydroxychloroquine regularly. Avoid sun exposure — use sunscreen and protective clothing. Get regular kidney and blood monitoring.

differential_questions: Do you have thick, red patches with silvery scales on your skin?
Are the patches on your elbows, knees, or scalp?
Do any joints ache or swell?
Does anything trigger flare-ups — stress, infections, medications?
psoriasis.patient_education: Keep skin moisturized. Follow your treatment plan consistently. Avoid triggers like stress and skin injuries. Get regular skin checks.

differential_questions: Have you noticed any loss of peripheral vision?
Do you have a family history of glaucoma?
Have you had eye pain, headaches, or seeing halos around lights?
When was your last eye exam?
glaucoma.patient_education: Use eye drops consistently — missed doses can increase eye pressure. Get regular eye exams. Glaucoma is treatable but not curable, so ongoing monitoring is essential.

differential_questions: Do you have widespread body pain lasting more than 3 months?
Do you feel exhausted even after sleeping?
Do you have difficulty concentrating or memory problems ('fibro fog')?
Are there specific tender points on your body that are painful to press?
fibromyalgia.patient_education: Regular gentle exercise (walking, swimming) is one of the best treatments. Stress management and good sleep habits are essential. Medication can help but won't cure the condition.

differential_questions: Have you ever used injectable drugs?
Did you receive a blood transfusion before 1992?
Have you noticed yellowing of your skin or eyes?
Have you had any abnormal liver tests?
hepatitis C.patient_education: New antiviral treatments can cure hepatitis C in most cases. Avoid alcohol. Get vaccinated against hepatitis A and B. Regular liver monitoring is important.

differential_questions: Have you had periods of feeling extremely energetic or euphoric?
During these high periods, did you need less sleep or take unusual risks?
Have you had periods of deep depression following the high periods?
Is there a family history of bipolar disorder or mood problems?
bipolar disorder.patient_education: Take mood stabilizers consistently — don't stop during stable periods. Maintain a regular sleep schedule. Avoid alcohol and recreational drugs. Therapy alongside medication gives best results.

differential_questions: Have you experienced or witnessed a traumatic event?
Do you have recurrent nightmares or flashbacks about the event?
Do you avoid situations that remind you of the trauma?
Are you easily startled or constantly on guard?
PTSD.patient_education: Trauma-focused therapy (like CPT or PE) is highly effective. Medications can help manage symptoms. Recovery takes time — be patient with yourself.

differential_questions: Which joints are affected? Is it the knees, hips, or hands?
Does the pain worsen with activity and improve with rest?
Do you have morning stiffness that resolves within 30 minutes?
Have you noticed any bony enlargements in your finger joints?
ostearthritis.patient_education: Stay active with low-impact exercise (swimming, cycling). Weight loss reduces joint stress. Use heat/cold for pain relief. Don't overuse NSAIDs — they can affect stomach and kidneys.
